questions.promptTex: \text{Compute } P(8, 3).
steps: $P(n, r) = n(n-1)(n-2)\cdots$ ($r$ terms).

questions.promptTex: \text{Compute } C(8, 3).
steps: $C(n, r) = \dfrac{P(n, r)}{r!}$.

questions.promptTex: \text{From 10 candidates, how many ways to elect a president and vice president (distinct positions)?}
questions.explanation: Distinct positions → order matters → permutation. $P(10, 2) = 10 \cdot 9 = 90$.
steps: Order matters (president ≠ vice president) → permutation.

questions.promptTex: \text{From 10 candidates, pick a 2-person committee (no titles).} \\ \text{Number of committees?}
hints: No titles → ORDER doesn't matter → combination.
questions.explanation: No titles → order doesn't matter → combination. $C(10, 2) = 45$.
steps: Order doesn't matter (Alice + Bob = Bob + Alice) → combination.

questions.promptTex: \text{How many ways to arrange the letters of WORD?}
questions.explanation: $4! = 24$ arrangements.
steps: 4 distinct letters → all permutations: $4! = 24$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Compute } 6!.
steps: $6 \cdot 5 \cdot 4 \cdot 3 \cdot 2 \cdot 1 = 720$.

questions.promptTex: \text{How many 5-card poker hands from a 52-card deck?}
steps: Poker hand: order of dealing doesn't change the hand → combination.
$C(52, 5) = \dfrac{52!}{5! \cdot 47!} = \dfrac{52 \cdot 51 \cdot 50 \cdot 49 \cdot 48}{5!}$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Race with 12 runners. Top 3 get gold, silver, bronze.} \\ \text{Number of possible podium outcomes?}
steps: Different medal positions = order matters → permutation.

questions.promptTex: \text{Lottery: pick 6 numbers from 1 to 49. Number of possible tickets?}
questions.explanation: Lottery numbers are typically unordered → $C(49, 6)$.
steps: Lottery winning numbers don't depend on the order they were drawn — order doesn't matter.
$C(49, 6) = 13{,}983{,}816$ — about 14 million possible tickets.

questions.promptTex: \text{Calculate } \binom{6}{4}.
steps: Use the symmetry $\binom{n}{k} = \binom{n}{n-k}$: $\binom{6}{4} = \binom{6}{2}$.

questions.promptTex: \text{In how many ways can you arrange 7 books on a shelf if 2 specific books must be together?}
hints: Treat the 2 books as a single unit → 6 things to arrange = 6!. Then × 2! for ordering of the pair.
questions.explanation: Glue the 2 books together → 6 arrangeable units = $6! = 720$. Times $2!$ for internal order = $1440$.
steps: Group the 2 special books as one 'super-book'.
Now we're arranging 6 things (5 books + 1 super-book): $6! = 720$ ways.
Within the super-book, 2 ways to order the pair: $\times 2! = 2$.

questions.promptTex: \text{From 8 boys and 6 girls, choose a committee of 3 boys and 2 girls.} \\ \text{Number of committees?}
hints: Choose 3 boys from 8: $C(8, 3) = 56$. Choose 2 girls from 6: $C(6, 2) = 15$.
steps: Two independent choices: pick boys, then pick girls. Multiply.
Boys: $C(8, 3) = \dfrac{8 \cdot 7 \cdot 6}{6} = 56$.
Girls: $C(6, 2) = \dfrac{6 \cdot 5}{2} = 15$.
Total: $56 \cdot 15 = 840$ committees.

choices: Choose 5 students from 20 for a committee
Arrange 5 students in a line for a photo
Pick a hand of 5 cards from a deck
Pick 5 lottery numbers (where order of drawing doesn't matter)
hints: Permutation = order matters. Photo lineup has distinct left-to-right positions.
Committees, hands of cards, and lottery picks are typically unordered (combinations).
questions.explanation: A photo lineup has *distinct positions* — 'Alice next to Bob' looks different from 'Bob next to Alice'. Order matters → permutation. Committees, card hands, and lottery picks: swapping members doesn't change the result → combinations.
steps: **The test.** Ask: 'If I swap two of the chosen items, is the answer the same?' Yes → combination. No → permutation.

**Apply.** Committee of 5: swap Alice and Bob in 'committee = {Alice, Bob, ...}' — same committee. Combination. Photo line: swap Alice and Bob in 'line: Alice, Bob, Charlie' vs 'line: Bob, Alice, Charlie' — different photo. Permutation.
**Mnemonic.** *Arrangements* and *sequences* (lineups, codes, rankings) → permutation. *Selections* and *groups* (teams, hands, sets) → combination.

questions.promptTex: \text{There are 8 people at a party. Each person shakes hands with every other person exactly once.} \\ \text{How many handshakes occur?}
hints: A handshake is an *unordered pair* — order doesn't matter.
questions.explanation: Each handshake involves an unordered pair of two distinct people. Number of such pairs: $\binom{8}{2} = 28$.
steps: **Recognize the type.** A handshake is an unordered pair $\{A, B\}$ with $A \neq B$. Counting pairs from $n$ people = $\binom{n}{2}$.
**Compute.** $\binom{8}{2} = \dfrac{8 \cdot 7}{2} = 28$.
**Why not 8×7?** $8 \cdot 7 = 56$ counts ORDERED pairs (Alice→Bob and Bob→Alice as two different handshakes). Each handshake double-counted → divide by 2.

**Pattern lesson — handshakes everywhere.** Number of edges in a complete graph on $n$ vertices = $\binom{n}{2}$. Same count covers: 2-element subsets, line segments between $n$ points, games in a round-robin tournament, etc.

questions.promptTex: \text{How many diagonals does a regular octagon have?} \\ \text{(A diagonal connects two NON-adjacent vertices.)}
questions.explanation: Total segments between any 2 of 8 vertices: $\binom{8}{2} = 28$. Of these, exactly 8 are sides of the octagon. Diagonals: $28 - 8 = 20$.
steps: **Count all vertex pairs.** Each pair of vertices defines a segment. $\binom{8}{2} = 28$.
**Subtract the sides.** An octagon has 8 sides. Each side connects two ADJACENT vertices.
**Diagonals = pairs minus sides.** $28 - 8 = 20$.
**General formula.** $n$-gon diagonals = $\binom{n}{2} - n = \dfrac{n(n-3)}{2}$. For $n = 8$: $8 \cdot 5 / 2 = 20$ ✓.

questions.promptTex: \text{Arrange the letters of TRIANGLE (8 distinct letters) so that all 3 vowels (I, A, E) are together. How many arrangements?}
hints: Treat the 3 vowels as a single block. Now there are 5 consonants + 1 block = 6 entities to arrange.
Inside the block, 3 vowels can be ordered $3! = 6$ ways.
questions.explanation: Block trick. Treat IAE as one super-letter; now 5 consonants + 1 block = 6 entities. Arrange in $6! = 720$ ways. Then the 3 vowels inside the block can be ordered $3! = 6$ ways. $720 \cdot 6 = 4320$.
steps: **Recognize the type — 'together' constraint.** Standard technique: bundle the constrained items into a single 'super-item', count arrangements of the new collection, then multiply by internal arrangements of the bundle.
**Bundle the vowels.** Treat I, A, E as one inseparable block. We now arrange: T, R, N, G, L (5 consonants) + 1 block = 6 entities.
**Arrange the 6 entities.** $6! = 720$.
**Internal arrangements of the bundle.** Inside the IAE block, the 3 vowels permute in $3! = 6$ ways.
**Multiply (independent choices).** $720 \cdot 6 = 4320$ total arrangements.

**Why the technique works.** The block constraint reduces a complicated 'arrange with adjacency' problem to a clean 'arrange 6 items' problem. Then independence between block-placement and within-block-arrangement lets you multiply.

questions.promptTex: \text{From 5 men and 4 women, choose a committee of 4 with AT LEAST 2 women.} \\ \text{Number of committees?}
hints: Three cases (mutually exclusive): exactly 2 W, exactly 3 W, exactly 4 W.
questions.explanation: 'At least 2 women' = 2 women OR 3 women OR 4 women. Three mutually exclusive cases. Sum: $60 + 20 + 1 = 81$ committees.
steps: **Recognize the type — 'at least k' on combinations.** Enumerate disjoint cases by exact count, then add.
**Case 2 women, 2 men.** Choose 2 women from 4 AND 2 men from 5: $\binom{4}{2} \cdot \binom{5}{2} = 6 \cdot 10 = 60$.
**Case 3 women, 1 man.** $\binom{4}{3} \cdot \binom{5}{1} = 4 \cdot 5 = 20$.
**Case 4 women, 0 men.** $\binom{4}{4} \cdot \binom{5}{0} = 1 \cdot 1 = 1$.
**Add (mutually exclusive cases).** $60 + 20 + 1 = 81$.

**Alternative — complement.** $\binom{9}{4} = 126$ total. Subtract committees with 0 or 1 women: $\binom{5}{4}\binom{4}{0} + \binom{5}{3}\binom{4}{1} = 5 + 40 = 45$. So $126 - 45 = 81$ ✓. Same answer, different work. When 'at-least' has many cases, complement is often shorter.

questions.promptTex: \text{Compute } \binom{20}{18} \text{ using the symmetry of binomial coefficients.}
questions.explanation: Symmetry of binomial coefficients: $\binom{n}{k} = \binom{n}{n-k}$. So $\binom{20}{18} = \binom{20}{2} = 20 \cdot 19 / 2 = 190$.
steps: **Recognize the trick — symmetry.** $\binom{n}{k} = \binom{n}{n-k}$. Computing $\binom{20}{18}$ directly involves 18 factors; computing $\binom{20}{2}$ involves 2. Same answer, vastly less work.
**Why is the symmetry true?** Choosing 18 of 20 items is equivalent to choosing the 2 to LEAVE OUT. Same set of partitions, counted two ways.
**Compute the easier form.** $\binom{20}{2} = \dfrac{20 \cdot 19}{2!} = \dfrac{380}{2} = 190$.
**Pattern lesson — always use the smaller $k$.** When computing $\binom{n}{k}$ by hand and $k > n/2$, switch to $\binom{n}{n-k}$ for easier arithmetic.

questions.promptTex: \text{Number of distinct arrangements of the letters of BALLOON?}
hints: 7 letters total. L appears 2 times, O appears 2 times. B, A, N each once.
questions.explanation: Letters in BALLOON: B, A, L, L, O, O, N — 7 total with L appearing 2 times and O appearing 2 times. Divide $7!$ by the factorials of repeat counts: $7!/(2! \cdot 2!) = 5040/4 = 1260$.
steps: **Recognize the type — arrangements with identical letters.** When some letters repeat, divide $n!$ by the factorial of each repeat-count.
**Catalog the letters.** BALLOON: 7 letters. B × 1, A × 1, L × 2, O × 2, N × 1.
**Apply the formula.** Total arrangements = $\dfrac{n!}{a! \cdot b! \cdots}$ where $a, b, \ldots$ are the repeat-counts.

**Why divide by repeat-factorials?** Treating the two L's as distinct gives $7! = 5040$ orderings, but each true arrangement gets counted $2!$ times (swapping the two L's gives the same word). Similarly for the two O's. So divide by $2! \cdot 2! = 4$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Pascal's triangle identity: } \binom{n}{r-1} + \binom{n}{r} = ?
hints: This is the recurrence that BUILDS Pascal's triangle: each entry is the sum of the two above it.
questions.explanation: Pascal's identity: $\binom{n}{r-1} + \binom{n}{r} = \binom{n+1}{r}$. This is exactly the rule that builds Pascal's triangle row by row — each entry is the sum of the two entries directly above it.
steps: **Recall how Pascal's triangle is constructed.** Each row starts and ends with 1; every other entry is the sum of the two diagonally-above entries.
**Reading entries as binomials.** Row $n$, position $r$ (zero-indexed) holds $\binom{n}{r}$. The two entries above are $\binom{n-1}{r-1}$ and $\binom{n-1}{r}$.
**Equivalent form.** Sliding by one: $\binom{n}{r-1} + \binom{n}{r} = \binom{n+1}{r}$.

**Combinatorial proof.** $\binom{n+1}{r}$ counts $r$-element subsets of an $(n+1)$-set. Split into 'subsets containing element $x$' (choose remaining $r-1$ from $n$: $\binom{n}{r-1}$) plus 'subsets not containing $x$' (choose all $r$ from remaining $n$: $\binom{n}{r}$). Adds to $\binom{n+1}{r}$.

questions.promptTex: \text{From 10 people, choose a 3-person committee.} \\ \text{How many committees do NOT include person X (a specific person)?}
hints: If X is excluded, you're choosing from the other 9 people.
questions.explanation: Excluding person X leaves 9 candidates. Choose 3 from 9: $\binom{9}{3} = 84$.
steps: **Recognize — 'must exclude' constraint.** Just shrink the pool. X is out, so we choose from the remaining $n - 1 = 9$ people.
**Compute.** $\binom{9}{3} = \dfrac{9 \cdot 8 \cdot 7}{6} = \dfrac{504}{6} = 84$.

**Sanity check via complement.** Total committees: $\binom{10}{3} = 120$. Committees that INCLUDE X (one slot used, choose 2 more from 9): $\binom{9}{2} = 36$. Committees excluding X: $120 - 36 = 84$ ✓.

**Pattern lesson.** 'Must include X' = pre-place X, choose remaining from pool − 1: $\binom{n-1}{r-1}$. 'Must exclude X' = remove X from pool: $\binom{n-1}{r}$. These two count complementary cases of $\binom{n}{r}$.

questions.promptTex: \text{There are 8 points in a plane, no three of which are collinear.} \\ \text{How many distinct triangles can be drawn using these points as vertices?}
hints: Each triangle = a choice of 3 points (order doesn't matter; collinearity ruled out).
questions.explanation: Each triangle is determined by a choice of 3 points. With 8 points (no 3 collinear), every 3-point subset gives a valid triangle: $\binom{8}{3} = 56$.
steps: **Recognize — triangles correspond to 3-point subsets.** A triangle is a set of 3 vertices (order doesn't matter). The 'no 3 collinear' condition guarantees every 3-subset is a genuine triangle (not degenerate).
**Compute.** $\binom{8}{3} = \dfrac{8 \cdot 7 \cdot 6}{3!} = \dfrac{336}{6} = 56$.

**What if some are collinear?** Then those collinear triples don't form triangles. Subtract the bad cases. E.g., if 4 specific points are collinear: bad triples = $\binom{4}{3} = 4$. Triangles = $\binom{8}{3} - 4 = 52$.
**Pattern lesson.** Geometric counting problems often reduce to subset counting, with constraints handled by subtracting degenerate cases.
